coredata.dc:title: A comparative study on antibacterial activity of sulfur- and phosphorus- doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Kang
Sun, Wanlin
Chi, Yuting
Chai, Shuiqin
Sun, Chengyu
Wu, Wen
coredata.dc:description: Effectively killing microbial pathogens without inducing resistance is a challenge, especially in the treatment of infectious diseases. In this study, sulfur (S)- and phosphorus (P)-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using m-aminophenol as carbon source, thiourea and phosphoric acid as S and P dopants, respectively. Compared with S-CQDs, P doping could lead to a red-shift of the maximum emission wavelength. In addition, the as-prepared S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs exhibited unique antibacterial efficacy against both E. coli and S. aureus without developing drug resistance. In contrast, the S, P-CQDs had no preference for Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, and they had a somewhat reduced bactericidal efficacy than that of S-CQDs, but S, P-CQDs had better bacteriostatic stability than S-CQDs. Such an excellent antibacterial effect might be attributed to the generation of a large number of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), which disrupted the structure and the regular surface of bacterial cell. Consequently, the prepared S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs could be applied as antibacterial drugs with broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and without drug resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Elemental doping
Antibacterial activity
Drug resistance
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs
Antioxidant activity of CQDs determined by DPPH• assay
Antibacterial activity of S-CQDs, and S, P -CQDs
The biocompatibility of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by Scientific Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (Grant Number KJQN201901501 ) and Research Foundation of Chongqing University of Science and Technology (Grant Number ckrc2019031 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM (inset: particle size distribution and HRTEM images) images of the S-CQDs (a), S, P-CQDs (b); and AFM images (inset: the height profile) of the S-CQD (c), S, P -CQD (d).
XPS full spectra of S-CQDs (a), S, P-CQDs (e), high-resolution spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) of S-CQDs, high-resolution spectra of C1s (f), N1s (g), O1s (h) and P 2p (i) of S, P-CQDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of the two kinds of CQDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the two kinds of CQDs. (c)-(d) PL spectra of S-CQDs, S, P-CQDs under different excitation conditions.

Inhibition zone photos of the control group, gentamicin (4000 μg/mL), S-CQDs (20 mg/mL), S, P-CQDs (20 mg/mL) against E. coli (a) and S. aureus (b) (1.5 × 107 CFU/mL) on LB medium for 12 h and schematic diagram of the inhibition zone area.
Inhibitory effect on E. coli (a) (c) and S. aureus (b) (d) of different concentrations of S-CQDs (a, b) and S, P-CQDs (c, d), respectively.

The generated ROS of S-CQDs and S,P-CQDs. (a, b) The generated O2 •–. (c) The generated 1O2 of S-CQDs. (d) The generated 1O2 of S,P-CQDs. (e) The generated •OH of S-CQDs. (f) The generated •OH of S,P-CQDs.
SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus untreated and treated with S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs in LB broth medium for 12 h.
Studies on the resistance of S-CQDs (a) and S, P-CQDs (b) to E. coli and S. aureus, cellular toxicity of S-CQDs (c) and S, P-CQDs (d) on HCT 116 cells viability.
Schematic diagram of preparation and antibacterial application of S-CQDs, S, P-CQDs.
MIC of CQDs on different microbes.

head.ce:title: A comparative study on antibacterial activity of sulfur- and phosphorus- doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Medical and Pharmaceutical College, Chongqing 404120, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, Chongqing 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCollege of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, NO. 20 East Daxuecheng Road, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effectively killing microbial pathogens without inducing resistance is a challenge, especially in the treatment of infectious diseases. In this study, sulfur (S)- and phosphorus (P)-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using m-aminophenol as carbon source, thiourea and phosphoric acid as S and P dopants, respectively. Compared with S-CQDs, P doping could lead to a red-shift of the maximum emission wavelength. In addition, the as-prepared S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs exhibited unique antibacterial efficacy against both E. coli and S. aureus without developing drug resistance. In contrast, the S, P-CQDs had no preference for Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, and they had a somewhat reduced bactericidal efficacy than that of S-CQDs, but S, P-CQDs had better bacteriostatic stability than S-CQDs. Such an excellent antibacterial effect might be attributed to the generation of a large number of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), which disrupted the structure and the regular surface of bacterial cell. Consequently, the prepared S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs could be applied as antibacterial drugs with broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and without drug resistance.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The emergence of multi-drug resistant pathogens caused by the abuse of antibiotics has become a serious problem worldwide [1]. Since bacteria can quickly adapt to harsh environments and develop resistance to antibiotics, drug-resistant bacterial infections become highly challenging to treat in clinical medicine, and even directly lead to increased mortality [2–4]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop new antibacterial agents with antibacterial mechanisms different from that of antibiotics [5,6]. Due to their extremely small size and good biocompatibility, carbon-based nanomaterials have shown great potential in biomedicine, especially in the field of antibacterial research [7–9].

Among many carbon-based nanomaterials, CQDs have attracted attention due to their great water solubility, chemical stability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [10,11]. With a size of less than 10 nm, it is an excellent candidate in some fields such as biosensing, photodynamic therapy, drug delivery, biological imaging, and fingerprint imaging [12–17]. In recent years, CQDs have been reported as effective antibacterial agents, which have antibacterial activity by synergy of multiple mechanisms [18–20]. The cation effect is one of the most common antibacterial mechanisms of CQDs, in which positively charged CQDs electrostatically bind with bacteria, eventually leading to cell wall destruction and bacterial death [21,22]. CQDs sterilized by cationic effect, on the other hand, have no or very low bacteriostatic activity against gram-negative bacteria. Wang et al. [23] reported a series of cationic CQDs, which could selectively kill gram-positive bacteria by electrostatic interaction, but none of these CQDs possessed an inhibitory effect on gram-negative bacteria. This can be improved by antibacterial agents with other bacteriostatic mechanisms such as reactive oxygen species (ROS) induced oxidative stress [24], the interference of gene expression for bacterial cells [18], etc. Wu et al. [25] developed levofloxacin-based CQDs with a great antibacterial effect against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, which was attributed to membrane damage caused by ROS generation. Markovi´c et al. [26] synthesized a series of CQDs by gamma rays at different doses and all CQDs had antibacterial activity due to high level of singlet oxygen generation which penetrates the bacterial membrane and eradicates the bacteria. ROS are classified into two types: free radicals ROS such as superoxide (O2 •–), hydroxyl (•OH) and non-radical ROS such as singlet oxygen (1O2) or hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [27]. The short life cycle of these ROS reacts with cellular components in a non-specific way, resulting in substantial oxidative damage, and the damage caused by ROS suggests that it is extremely difficult to develop drug resistance [28]. Therefore, it is extremely desired to develop high-efficiency ROS-producing drug CQDs.

The structure and composition of CQDs can potentially be tuned by elemental doping, and elemental doping provides more electron active sites through changing the electron density of the CQDs system [29–31]. The nitrogen (N) atom is the most studied dopant because it has five valence electrons bound to carbon atoms. It provides numerous options for CQDs functionalization [32,33]. Similarly to N atoms, phosphorus (P) atoms can act as n-type donors to tune the structure of CQDs [34,35]. Furthermore, nano-sized sulfur (S) atoms are water soluble, biocompatible, and antimicrobial [36,37], making them a popular dopant atom.

Herein, we report two element-doped CQDs by a one-step hydrothermal method using m-aminophenol as the carbon source and thiourea and phosphoric acid as S and P dopants, respectively (Scheme 1 ). The morphology, size, structural composition, and optical properties are investigated. The results show that the as-prepared S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs exhibit uniform morphology, good water solubility, and fluorescence stability. Furthermore, the S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs are highly lethal to Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) but only have low cytotoxicity. The antibacterial mechanism of CQDs may be attributed to ROS generation, which disrupts the structure and the regular surface of bacterial cell.

Thiourea and m-aminophenol were purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd. Macklin Reagent Co. Ltd. provided 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT). Sodium chloride, yeast extract, tryptone and agar were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water (18 MΩ) was used in all the experiments.

The morphologies of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs were carried out by using a JEOL 2100F (Tokyo, Japan) transmission electron microscope. Dimension Icon Scan Asyst atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Co., Karlsruhe, Germany) was used to determine the height distribution of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs. Functional group message of the specimen surfaces was performed with a Nicolet iS5 Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). The elemental composition of CQDs was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) equipped with an ESCALAB Xi+ spectrometer. The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra were measured by using an evolution 220 ultraviolet-visible spectrometer. The morphology of microorganisms was measured under a Hitachi S-3400 N scanning electron microscope (SEM, Tokyo, Japan). The PL spectra were monitored with a Shimatzu RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For sulfur-doped CQDs (S-CQDs), 0.20 g m-aminophenol and 0.10 g thiourea was dispersed in 5.0 mL ultrapure water, and then the mixture was transferred to a 25 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and kept at 220 °C for 48 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, then the suspension was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min at room temperature and dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (100–500 Da) for 48 h for purification. For phosphorus and sulfur co-doped CQDs (S, P-CQDs), 0.20 g m-aminophenol, 0.10 g thiourea and 1 mL phosphoric acid was dispersed in 4.0 mL ultrapure water. The subsequent treatments were consistent with those for S-CQDs. Finally, both CQDs solutions were freeze-dried and kept in cold stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of CQDs determined by DPPH• assay
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were immersed in 1.5 mL 100 μM DPPH• ethanol solution and incubated in dark for 5 days. The absorption intensity of DPPH• was detected at 518 nm in predetermined time intervals (30 min, 1 h, 2 h, first day, second day, third day and fifth day). The radical scavenging activity (RSA) of CQDs was calculated from the formula: RSA (%) = (Ac‒ACQDs) / Ac × 100, where Ac was the absorbance of DPPH• solution at 518 nm, ACQDs was the absorbance in the presence of the CQDs samples.

The broth microdilution method was used to determine the MIC of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs. In lysogeny broth (LB) liquid media, E. coli and S. aureus were cultured overnight at 37 °C with agitation at 180 rpm, and subsequently diluted to a concentration of 1.5 × 107 CFU/mL. 100 μL of bacterial suspension was added to the wells of a sterile 96-well cell culture plate already containing 100 μL various concentrations of S-CQDs or S, P-CQDs. Negative control wells were treated with a bacterial suspension in LB medium, and LB medium diluted with sterile water was used as blank. The treated samples were cultured at 37 °C for 12 h. At the end of incubation, 200 μL of mixture was incubated with 10 μL of MTT for an additional 20 min.

Logarithmic growth phase cells of E. coli and S. aureus bacterial suspension (1.5 × 107 CFU/mL) were treated with CQDs at concentrations of 0.48 mg/mL (S, P-CQDs), 0.192 mg/mL (S-CQDs). Bacteria incubated in LB broth were used as the control groups. Next, bacterial broth in liquid medium was removed by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) and washed three times with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4). The washed bacterial cells were fixed in 5% glutaraldehyde for 4 h at 4 °C. Then the bacteria were soaked in a range of ethanol solutions (25%, 50%, 75%, 90%, and 100%) to dehydrate. The sample morphology was observed on a Hitachi S-3400 N scanning electron microscope after being dried by vacuum and gold spraying.

As mentioned above, we have obtained the initial MIC values of S-CQDs, S, P-CQDs against E. coli and S. aureus. Subsequently, the bacteria in the tube at a concentration of 1/2 MIC were diluted (1.5 × 107 CFU/mL) for the next experiment. The treated bacterial suspensions were incubated with different concentrations of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs solutions at 37 °C for 24 h to obtain new MIC. The test was repeated each day for 14 passages [25].

HCT 116 cells were selected to evaluate the toxicity of CQDs by the MTT assay. The HCT 116 cells were plated on a 96-well cell culture plate at 1.5 × 105 cells per mL and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. The medium was then replaced with 100 µL of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) medium supplemented with 2% fetal bovine serum (FBS), and CQDs were added to the medium at concentrations from 0 to 1000 g/mL, and the cells were nurtured at 37 °C for 24 h. The mixture was flushed three times with PBS buffer to remove CQDs. Then 10 μL MTT dye and 100 μL DMEM were added to each well, and the cells were incubated at 37 °C for 2 h in the dark. Finally, the viable cells were detected by a microplate reader (Victor III, Perkin-Elmer). The untreated cells were used as a blank control with 100% cell surface viability. The experiment was repeated six times to obtain the average value.

To assess whether CQDs generate superoxide (O2 −), a chemical probe chlorinated nitrotetrazolium blue (NBT) was employed, which reacts with O2 − to form water-insoluble dimethylformazan. Briefly, 1 mL of an aqueous solution containing NBT and 1 mg/mL CQDs was transferred to a cuvette, UV–vis spectra were periodically recorded and the formation of xylene was monitored at 259 nm. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was carried out to detect the 1O2 and •OH generation from CQDs by using 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and 5‑tert-butoxycarbonyl-5-methyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (BMPO), respectively. Briefly, 0.1 mL CQDs solution were mixed with 3 μL TEMP or BMPO and then transferred into a capillary tube. The ESR spectra were collected before and after laser irradiation.

The CQDs particles were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). By measuring more than 50 particles, the particle size distribution of S-CQDs was calculated to range from 4.6 nm to 7.8 nm with an average particle size of 6.20 nm and a lattice spacing of approximately 0.29 nm (Fig. 1 a). The AFM was further employed to measure the thickness of S-CQDs, which were mainly distributed in a narrow range of 4.70–7.63 nm (Fig. 1c). Similarly, TEM showed that the size distribution of S, P-CQDs varied from 4.2 nm to 7.4 nm with an average size of 5.59 nm, and HRTEM images showed that the S, P-CQDs had a lattice spacing of about 0.28 nm (Fig. 1b). The thickness distribution of S, P-CQDs was between 4.63 to 6.09 nm in AFM (Fig. 1d). The above results indicate that these CQDs exhibit a uniform spherical morphology [23,34].

The elemental components and surface states of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs were evaluated by the XPS analysis. The full-scan XPS spectra of these CQDs (Fig. 2 a, e) showed the elemental contents of C 26.80%, O 65.03%, N 5.13%, S 3.04% for S-CQDs, respectively, and C 30.45%, O 42.41%, N 3.83%, S 2.84%, P 20.47% for S, P-CQDs, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of S-CQDs (Fig. 2b) and S, P-CQDs (Fig. 2f) showed two peaks, including 284.8 (CC/C=C bonds), 286.3 eV (CO/N/P/S bonds) [28,38]. For S-CQDs, the N 1 s spectra showed the existence of CN, C=N and NH at the peaks of 399.1, 400.8 and 401.8 eV (Fig. 2c). For S, P-CQDs, the N 1 s spectra showed two peaks at 399.3, 401.2 eV (Fig. 2g), which were attributed to NH, and CN [14,21]. While the O 1 s spectra of S-CQDs (Fig. 2d) showed the existence of C=O, CO at the peaks of 532.05, 533.05, respectively. The O 1 s spectra of S, P-CQDs (Fig. 2h) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 530.1, 530.8, 532 eV, which could be ascribed to O=C, CO, and ON/S/P [32,39]. The S 2p spectra of S-CQDs (Fig. 1Sa) at binding energy 162.30 and 169. 6 eV indicated the presence of C−S and SO4 2−, respectively. The S 2p spectra of S, P-CQDs (Fig. 1Sb) at the peaks of 167.2 and 168.5 eV indicated the presence of C−SO2 and C−SO3 [40]. The P 2p spectra of S, P-CQDs (Fig. 2i) was well-fitted into P-O bonds (133.3 eV) and P-C bonds (134.3 eV) [14].

Further characterization of CQDs was conducted by FT-IR (Fig. 3 a). Broad strong bands around 3316 cm−1 for S-CQDs and 3147 cm−1 for S, P-CQDs could be assigned the stretching vibrations of OH/NH. The peaks at 2062 cm−1 for S-CQDs and 2387 cm−1 for S, P-CQDs originated from C ≡ C or C=C‒C=C vibrations. The peak around 1600 cm−1 originated from C=O stretching vibrations of carboxylate group. The peaks between 1400 and 1490 cm−1 could be due to the CN stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1155 cm−1 for S-CQDs and 1148 cm−1 for S,P-CQDs originated from CO/C-S stretching vibrations [41]. In contrast, the spectra of S, P-CQDs suggested the presence of P-OR (R=C, N, P) at 989 cm−1 on the surface, indicating the merging of P element into the S,P-CQDs [37,42].

The UV-visible absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs were investigated for comparison. The S-CQDs exhibited an absorption peak at 279 nm due to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains. The S, P-CQDs had two clear absorption peaks at 257 with a shoulder band at 273 nm and 385 nm, which attributed to the π–π* transition of C=C and n→π* transition of the surface groups, respectively (Fig. 3b) [41]. The PL spectra (Fig. 3c, d) showed that the maximum emission of the two CQDs was red-shifted with the excitation wavelength increased, which demonstrated that the prepared CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent behavior. In addition, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of S-CQD were 290 nm and 382 nm, respectively. When doping the P element in the CQDs, the excitation and emission wavelengths of S, P -CQDs were 400 nm and 509 nm. The above results demonstrated that doping with P elements caused a red-shift of the maximum emission wavelength.

The effects of illumination time, oxidation degree, ionic strength and pH on the fluorescence stability of CQDs were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the PL and UV–vis absorption intensity of S-CQDs had not obviously change under continuous 24 h illumination, indicating that the S-CQDs had excellent photostability. The S,P-CQDs displayed excellent photostability in 4 h illumination, but PL and absorption intensity of S,P-CQDs gradually dropped as the illumination time extended to 24 h. Although the intensity of S,P-CQDs was reduced, it did not exceed 30%. Overall, the S-CQDs have better photostability than S,P-CQDs. In addition, these CQDs also showed good antioxidation properties, and the PL intensity of CQDs decreased only slightly even when the concentration of H2O2 reached 1000 μM (Fig. S3a). It was clear that the PL intensity of these CQDs was independent of pH ranging from 2.09 to 11.92 (Fig. S3b). The PL intensity of these CQDs negligibly decreased with an increasing concentration of NaCl concentration (up to 2.0 M), indicating that these CQDs were stable in high ionic strength solutions (Fig. S3c). Furthermore, the antioxidant activity of S-CQDs and S,P-CQDs was determined using a simple and quick DPPH• assay according to reported literature [43]. The control sample exhibited negligible antioxidant activity, while the absorbance of DPPH• solution with S-CQDs and S,P-CQDs decreased in 5 days (Fig. S4a). RSA showed an increasing tendency during all testing days (Fig. S4b). The results indicated that S-CQDs and S,P-CQDs can be considered as the effective antioxidant agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of S-CQDs, and S, P -CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of S-CQDs and S, P -CQDs was assessed by Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus. The MIC values were determined by the microdilution method, and live bacteria were stained with MTT after treated with different concentrations of CQDs (Fig. S5). As shown in Table 1 , the MIC values of S-CQDs and S, P -CQDs were 0.192 mg/mL and 0.48 mg/mL for E. coli, respectively, and 0.12 mg/mL and 0.48 mg/mL for S. aureus, respectively. The results showed that both two CQDs exhibited high antibacterial activity, and the inhibitory activity of S-CQDs was slightly stronger than that of S, P-CQDs. Furthermore, S, P-CQDs showed no selectivity while the S-CQDs showed stronger inhibitory activity to S. aureus than E. coli.

In addition, the inhibition zone test was adopted to probe the antibacterial effect of CQDs on different types of microorganism. The antibacterial effect of different samples against E. coli and S. aureus was shown (Fig. 4 ), including S-CQDs (20 mg/mL), S, P-CQDs (20 mg/mL), ultrapure water (as a negative control) and gentamicin (as a positive control). The results showed that no bacteriostatic circles were generated in all the negative controls. Moreover, the bacteriostatic diameters of S-CQDs were significantly larger than that of S, P-CQDs, indicating the antibacterial activity of S-CQDs against different bacteria was higher than that of S, P–CQDs, which was consistent with the results of the MIC value determination.

The time-dependent antimicrobial effect at different concentrations of CQDs was studied by recording OD600. The results (Fig. 5 ) showed that bacterial growth was inhibited to a certain extent when the bacteria were cultured with the two CQDs at concentrations of 0.5 MIC. When the bacteria were treated with 1 MIC concentration of S-CQDs, the growth of both bacteria was significantly inhibited only in the first 10 h. However, the bacteria were completely inhibited within 24 h after incubated with 1MIC concentration of S, P-CQDs, indicating that although the bacteriostatic activity of S-CQDs was better than that of S, P –CQDs, the bacteriostatic stability of the CQDs was improved after doping phosphorus to CQDs.

The generation of ROS in the presence of CQDs is one of the main causes of bacterial cell death. Therefore, the generation of four types of ROS was measured: singlet oxygen (1O2), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide (O2 •–), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [44,45]. The absorbance of NBT at 259 nm decreases when the deoxidation of NBT by O2 •– leads to the formation of diformazan. The results showed the UV absorption of NBT at 259 nm decreased in the presence of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs, obviously indicating S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs can generate O2 •– (Fig. 6 a,b). The ESR signals of TEMP and BMPO mixing with S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs were detected after 10 min of 635 nm laser irradiation, which indicated that these CQDs could simultaneously produce 1O2 and •OH under 635 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 6c,d,e,f). Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) catalyzes the oxidation of 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid (HPA) by H2O2 to produce fluorescent dimers (λem = 450 nm), which can be utilized as a reporter assay for H2O2 generation in solution. When performed in the presence of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs, no significant variations in fluorescence intensity at 450 nm were observed, suggesting the absence of H2O2 (Fig. S6).

SEM images of the CQD-treated microorganism further clarified the bactericidal ability of the two CQDs (Fig. 7 ). The control group showed morphologically intact. In contrast, after incubated with both CQDs, the morphology of the E. coli and S. aureus was destroyed, causing transudation of bacterial cytoplasm. Therefore, CQDs could penetrate the plasma membrane and cause cytoplasmic leakage, which may be related to the generation of ROS. Furthermore, the inhibition of the bacteria activity depended on the incubation time of CQDs with an elevated time to 30 min, 2 h and 6 h, S-CQDs caused the damage of cell structure of E. coli in 2 h while S,P-CQDs destroyed the morphology of E. coli only in 30 min (Fig. S7). Both of S-CQDs and S,P-CQDs could cause cytoplasmic leakage of S. aureus in 30 min (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The biocompatibility of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, antibiotic resistance is one of the most serious biosafety issues. To assess the potential of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs induced drug resistance to bacteria, E. coli and S. aureus at half inhibitory concentration were incubated with CQDs to survey MIC values for 14 passages. E. coli and S. aureus did not develop resistance to S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs (Fig. 8 a, b), suggesting that bacteria cannot easily trigger resistance to S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs. In addition, the vitro cytotoxicity of S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs was further evaluated by MTT test on HCT 116 cells. When the concentration of the two CQDs was as high as 500 μg/mL, the survival rates of HCT 116 cells remained above 80%, which indicated that S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs had insignificant toxicity to colon cancer cells in vitro (Fig. 8c, d).

In summary, two differently doped CQDs (S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs) were prepared and characterized. The as-prepared CQDs exhibit good luminescence stability, high solubility and low toxicity. Differently, the S, P-CQDs have no selectivity for Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria and their bactericidal ability is slightly lower than that of S-CQDs, but S, P-CQDs have better bacteriostatic stability than S-CQDs. What's more, S-CQDs and S, P-CQDs destroy the cell membrane integrity of E. coli and S. aureus and lead to the mortality of the bacteria, which might be attributed to the generation of a large number of intracellular ROS from CQDs. This finding may open a gateway for developing a new antimicrobial agent through the synthesis of multi-doped CQDs.

This research was funded by Scientific Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (Grant Number KJQN201901501) and Research Foundation of Chongqing University of Science and Technology (Grant Number ckrc2019031).

Kang Luo: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Wanlin Sun: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yuting Chi: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Shuiqin Chai: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Chengyu Sun: Formal analysis, Software, Writing – review & editing. Wen Wu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors disclosed no relevant relationships.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.136525.
